resources.string: How to Drink Water Properly
Drink in small amounts throughout the day, not all at once
Don’t wait until you’re extremely thirsty to drink.
Avoid drinking a large amount of water at once, as it can cause discomfort and, in people with underlying conditions, even lead to electrolyte imbalances.
Recommended daily water intake
Around 30–35 ml per kilogram of body weight per day.
If you are very active, the weather is hot, or you sweat a lot → you’ll need to increase your intake.
If you have heart, kidney, or blood pressure problems → you must consult your doctor before adjusting how much water you drink.
Best time to drink water
After waking up: 1 small glass of warm water to help “start up” your body.
Late morning and mid-afternoon: drink 1–2 small glasses, and don’t wait until you’re really thirsty.
30 minutes before meals: 1 small glass to support digestion (avoid drinking too much right before eating).
1–2 hours before bed: if you don’t usually wake up to urinate at night, you can drink a few sips; avoid drinking a lot right before sleeping.
What kind of water should you drink?
Best options:
Boiled and cooled water
Boiled and cooled water
Mineral water from a reliable source
Limit your intake of:
Sugary soft drinks / fizzy drinks.
Drinks that are very high in sugar (milk tea, bottled juices, etc.)
Coffee, tea, and similar drinks still count as part of your fluid intake, but they contain caffeine – so you shouldn’t count them 100% towards your daily water goal.
Listen to your body
Signs you may be dehydrated:
Dark yellow urine with a strong smell.
Dry lips, dry skin, lightheadedness, fatigue
Urinating very little during the day
Signs you may be drinking too much water (especially in a short period):
Frequent urination with very clear, water-like urine.
Feeling bloated, nauseous, having headaches, or feeling faint.
→ If you have underlying health conditions or notice anything unusual, you should see a doctor.
Be careful with these common water-intake mistakes.
Waiting until you’re THIRSTY to drink
By the time you feel thirsty, your body is already slightly dehydrated.
Problems: Can make you tired, give you headaches, and reduce your focus.
How to fix it:
Sip water regularly throughout the day instead of waiting until you’re thirsty:
After waking up: 1 small glass (200–300 ml).
Late morning, afternoon, evening: 1 small glass each time.
Drinking far less than your body needs
Only drinking 2–3 glasses a day, especially common in office workers sitting in air-conditioned rooms.
Problems: Dry skin, constipation, fatigue, peeing less often, dark yellow urine.
How to fix: Suggested daily water intake (total from plain water + soup + fruit):
On average: 30–40 ml per kg of body weight per day.
Example: 60 kg → about 1.8–2.4 litres/day.
If you exercise a lot or it’s very hot: increase your intake.
Drinking too much at once
Forgetting to drink all day, then “making up for it” at night by drinking 1–2 litres in one go.
Problems:
Overloads the kidneys and can dilute sodium in the blood (rare but dangerous).
Can cause bloating, indigestion, and nausea.
Break it up: drink about 150–250 ml at a time, several times throughout the day.
Drinking too close to mealtimes
Drinking 500–700 ml of water right before or right after a meal.
Problems: Dilutes stomach acid, may slow digestion and cause bloating (especially in people with sensitive stomachs).
30 minutes before a meal: 1 small glass (100–200 ml).
During the meal: sip small amounts if needed.
Avoid drinking large amounts immediately after a big meal.
Not drinking enough before, during, and after exercise
Going for a run, playing football, or working out at the gym and only realising afterwards that you didn’t drink anything the whole time.
Problems: Dehydration, muscle cramps, fatigue, low blood pressure, dizziness.
30–60 minutes before exercise: drink 200–300 ml.
During exercise: every 15–20 minutes, take a few small sips.
After exercise: rehydrate gradually, don’t gulp a large amount in one go.
Frequently drinking very cold/iced water
Loving ice-cold drinks all year round.
Can cause sudden blood vessel constriction in the throat, making sore throat and cough more likely.
For people with stomach issues, it can trigger spasms and stomach pain.
How to fix it: Go for cool or slightly chilled water, and avoid “ice-cold to the point your teeth hurt” all the time.
Using sugary drinks instead of plain water
Preferring soft drinks, milk tea, energy drinks, bottled juices… as your main fluids instead of water.
Raises blood sugar, increases the risk of weight gain and tooth decay.
Doesn’t hydrate as effectively as plain water.
Plain water should still be your main drink.
Sugary drinks: limit them and treat them like an occasional treat, not your everyday hydration source.
Ignoring the colour of your urine
Pale yellow, fairly clear: usually fine.
Dark yellow with a strong smell: you may be dehydrated (unless you’re taking certain medicines/vitamins).
Red, brown, or unusually cloudy: you should see a doctor.
Blindly believing “you must drink 3 litres a day”
Forcing yourself to follow a fixed amount (2–3 litres) without considering your body weight, activity level, or the weather. Everyone’s needs are different
The 30–40 ml/kg body weight formula.
Your sense of thirst
The colour of your urine
Drinking a lot of water right before bed
Drinking 500–700 ml of water just before sleeping because you think it’s “good for the kidneys and the skin”
You may need to wake up to have a wee, which disrupts your sleep.
Older adults or people with heart or kidney problems may experience swollen legs, puffiness, and discomfort.
1–2 hours before bed: you can have a small glass.
Right before sleep: just take a few sips if you feel thirsty.
Benefits of drinking lemon water
The light citrus flavour makes plain water more enjoyable, so many people naturally drink more throughout the day
Vitamin C boost
Lemons are a good source of vitamin C, an antioxidant that supports your immune system and helps protect cells from damage.
Supports digestion
Warm lemon water in the morning may stimulate digestion and help some people feel less bloated or heavy after meals.
Freshens breath
The acidic, citrusy taste can help reduce bad breath temporarily (but you should still rinse with plain water afterwards to protect your teeth).
May support skin health
Staying well hydrated plus getting some vitamin C can support collagen production, which is important for skin elasticity and overall skin appearance.
How water helps you sleep better
Keeps your body in balance
When you’re properly hydrated, your heart, circulation, and body temperature are more stable. This helps your body relax and shift more smoothly into sleep.
Reduces night-time cramps and discomfort
Mild dehydration can contribute to muscle cramps, headaches, dry mouth, or a scratchy throat at night — all of which can wake you up or make it harder to fall asleep.
Helps your body recover while you rest
Staying hydrated supports circulation and waste removal, helping your body recover during deep sleep.
Important note: Drink most of your fluids earlier in the day, and only sip a little at night—avoid large amounts right before bed to prevent waking up to wee.
Supports hormone and nervous system function

Water plays a role in how your body produces and regulates certain hormones and neurotransmitters. When you’re not dehydrated, processes that influence your sleep–wake cycle can work more efficiently.
Drinking water and weight loss
Helps control appetite
We often mistake thirst for hunger. Drinking a glass of water first helps you tell the difference, and having water 15–30 minutes before meals can help you feel full with smaller portions.
Replaces high-calorie drinks

Swapping sugary drinks (soft drinks, milk tea, sweetened coffee, juice) for plain water cuts a lot of “empty” calories. Over weeks and months, this reduction can make a real difference to your weight.
Supports metabolism and energy levels

Even mild dehydration can make you feel tired and less likely to move or exercise. Staying well hydrated helps your body work efficiently, supports normal metabolism, and makes it easier to stay active.
Helps digestion and reduces bloating
Drinking enough water helps your digestive system work smoothly and can reduce constipation and bloating, which sometimes makes people feel “heavier” than they really are.
Important reminder:
Eating a balanced, lower-calorie diet.
Moving more (exercise, walking, daily activity)
Sleeping and managing stress
Use water wisely: Drink water regularly, swap sugary drinks when possible, and have a glass before meals—without over drinking.
Drinking water and the digestive system
Helps start digestion in the mouth
Water is a key part of saliva, which helps soften food and begins breaking down carbohydrates. This makes swallowing easier and reduces strain on your oesophagus and stomach.
Supports stomach function
Your stomach needs enough fluid to mix food with stomach acid and digestive enzymes. This “mixture” helps break food into smaller pieces so nutrients can be absorbed more easily in the intestines.
Keeps things moving in the intestines
Water helps dissolve nutrients and carries them along the digestive tract. It also keeps the contents of your intestines soft and moving, reducing the risk of constipation.
Works together with fibre

Dietary fibre (from vegetables, fruit, whole grains) needs water to do its job properly. Without enough water, high-fibre meals can actually cause bloating and hard stools. With enough water, fibre helps form soft, bulky stools that are easier to pass.
Reduces constipation and bloating
When you’re dehydrated, your body pulls more water out of the stool, making it dry and hard. This can lead to constipation, straining, haemorrhoids, and a feeling of heaviness or bloating.
May ease heartburn in some people
Small sips of water may ease mild heartburn, but drinking too much with meals can worsen reflux for some—so stick to a comfortable, moderate amount.
Drink water regularly throughout the day
Have a small glass 15–30 minutes before meals.
During meals, sip slowly—don’t chug.
If you’re often bloated or constipated, increase both water and fibre together.
The effects of water on the brain and concentration
Maintains energy and alertness
The brain is ~70–75% water, so even mild dehydration can cause fatigue and brain fog. Staying hydrated helps you think clearly and feel more alert.
Supports focus and concentration
Dehydration can reduce focus and concentration, causing students, office workers, and drivers to get distracted and make more mistakes.
Helps mood and stress levels
Not drinking enough water can make you irritable, low, and more stressed. Staying hydrated helps your brain regulate mood more steadily.
Reduces headaches in some people
Not drinking enough water can trigger headaches for many people. While dehydration isn’t the cause of every headache, drinking regularly can help reduce mild dehydration-related headaches.
Supports memory and mental performance
Staying hydrated supports memory, reaction time, and focus—especially during exams or long work sessions.
Don’t rely on coffee or energy drinks alone. Keep water nearby and sip regularly—especially when studying, working, or driving. Pale yellow urine and steady energy are signs you’re well hydrated.
Impact of alcohol on your health
Short-term effects
Alcohol can slow your reactions and impair balance, increasing accident risk.
It may affect mood and behaviour (from relaxed to irritable or aggressive).
It also dehydrates you by increasing urination, leading to headaches, dry mouth, and hangovers.
Plus, it can irritate the stomach and cause nausea, vomiting, or pain.
Long-term effects (with frequent or heavy drinking)
Liver damage: Over time, alcohol can cause fatty liver, hepatitis, and eventually cirrhosis, where scar tissue replaces healthy liver tissue.
Heart and blood pressure problems: Heavy drinking over time can increase blood pressure, weaken the heart, and raise the risk of heart disease and stroke.
Brain and mental health: Long-term alcohol use can impair memory and focus, worsen mood (anxiety/depression), and increase addiction risk.

Digestive system and cancer risk: Heavy long-term alcohol use can damage the lining of the mouth, throat, stomach, and intestines, and increases the risk of several cancers (especially mouth, throat, liver, breast, and colon).
Immune system: Chronic heavy drinking can weaken your immune system, making it harder for your body to fight infections.
Weight and sleep

Alcohol adds “empty” calories that can lead to weight gain, especially with sugary mixers or late-night snacks. It may help you fall asleep faster, but it disrupts deep and REM sleep, reducing sleep quality.

Important note: Even low-to-moderate drinking isn’t risk-free. For some people (pregnancy, certain health conditions, specific medications, or a history of addiction), the safest choice is no alcohol.
Drinking water to reduce hormonal acne
Supports the body’s detox and balance
Your kidneys and liver process hormones and waste. Staying hydrated helps them work efficiently, which can support hormone balance and healthier skin.
Helps maintain skin barrier and moisture
Dehydration can make skin look dull, tight, and easily irritated. Staying hydrated supports the skin barrier and may reduce redness and acne-related irritation.
Supports blood circulation and nutrient delivery
Water helps keep blood flowing smoothly:
Nutrients, oxygen, and beneficial compounds are transported more efficiently to skin cells.
Waste products are carried away more effectively
This can contribute to a brighter, healthier-looking complexion over time.
May reduce dryness-related oiliness
For some people, when the skin is very dehydrated:
It may compensate by producing more oil
This can make skin look shiny but still feel dry underneath
Staying hydrated (plus using the right moisturiser) can help your skin feel more balanced and less “overcompensating.”
How to drink water to reduce facial oiliness
Supports skin hydration from the inside
Water helps maintain your body’s overall fluid balance. When you’re well hydrated:
Your skin is less likely to feel tight, rough, or flaky
Fine lines caused by dryness can appear softer.
Think of it as giving your skin a better “base” so your moisturiser and skincare routine work more effectively.
Helps the skin barrier function better
For some, very dehydrated skin can trigger the skin to produce more oil to compensate for dryness.
While water won’t turn off oily skin, staying hydrated plus using the right moisturiser can help your skin feel more balanced.
Helps with detox processes (indirectly)
Water itself doesn’t “detox” your skin like magic, but:
It helps your kidneys and liver work properly
These organs process waste products and by-products that, if not handled well, can influence inflammation in the body
Less internal “stress” can show up externally as calmer, more balanced skin.
Water-drinking mistakes that harm your skin
Drinking too little water all day
Chronic mild dehydration can make your skin look dull, tight, and more prone to fine lines caused by dryness. It can also make redness and irritation more noticeable.
Believing “water alone will fix my skin”

Water supports healthy skin, but it can’t replace sunscreen, a good skincare routine, balanced nutrition, sleep, and stress management. Relying only on drinking water and ignoring these factors can leave you disappointed.
Best times to drink water for smoother, clearer skin
Right after waking up
Have 1 small glass (200–300 ml) in the morning. This helps rehydrate your body after a night’s sleep and supports circulation and detox processes that affect your skin.
30 minutes before meals
Drink a small glass of water 20–30 minutes before eating.
This supports digestion, helps nutrients absorb better, and may prevent heavy, bloated feelings that can show on your face.
Between meals (not just with them)
Sip water between meals instead of only during meals.
Steady hydration helps keep the skin barrier more stable, reducing dryness and irritation.
Before and after exercise

Before exercise: drink a small glass of water. After exercise: rehydrate gradually. Good hydration during sweating and recovery helps blood flow and skin repair, giving you a healthier glow over time.
During long periods of screen time or study
When working or studying for hours, take a few sips every 30–60 minutes. This keeps circulation and focus steady, and prevents your skin from looking tired and dull.
1–2 hours before bed (but not too much right before)
You can drink a small glass 1–2 hours before sleeping.
Avoid gulping water just before bed so your sleep (and your skin’s nightly repair) isn’t disturbed by trips to the loo.
Drinking water during your period
Reduces bloating and water retention
Drinking enough water actually helps your body release excess fluid. This can ease that heavy, puffy feeling in your belly and legs.
Helps with cramps and headaches
Mild dehydration can make period cramps and headaches feel worse. Drinking water supports blood flow and muscle function, which may help reduce discomfort.
Supports mood and energy
When you’re low on water, you’re more likely to feel tired, irritable, and foggy.
Good hydration helps keep your energy and concentration more stable during your cycle.
Aids digestion
Many people experience constipation or digestive changes during their period. Drinking enough water (plus eating fibre) helps keep your bowels moving and reduces bloating.
Hydration for pregnant women
Supports blood volume and circulation

Supports blood volume and circulation. During pregnancy, your blood volume increases. Drinking enough water helps your heart and blood vessels work properly to deliver oxygen and nutrients to the baby.
Helps form amniotic fluid
Water contributes to the production of amniotic fluid, which cushions and protects your baby in the womb.
Reduces common pregnancy discomforts
Reduce constipation and haemorrhoids.
Ease swelling (water retention).
Lower the chance of headaches and dizziness
Supports kidney function
How much to drink (general guideline)
Pregnant women should aim for 2–2.5 litres of fluid per day, depending on body size, climate, and activity level.
This includes water, soups, milk, herbal teas, and water from foods (fruits, vegetables).
Pale yellow urine = usually well-hydrated
Dark yellow, strong smell = may need more fluids
Always follow your doctor’s or midwife’s advice, especially if you have conditions like pre-eclampsia, kidney problems, or gestational diabetes.
How much water should you drink in hot weather?
General guideline
Recommended fluid intake for healthy adults:
2–3 litres per day under normal conditions.
In hot weather, add 0.5–1 litre more, especially if sweating a lot.
Simple rule:
30–40 ml of water per kg of body weight per day, then add more if it\'s hot or you\'re active.
Example (hot weather):
60 kg person → base ~ 1.8 – 2.4L
Add 0.5 – 1L if working/exercising in the heat → Total ~ 2.3 – 3.4 L/day.

Instead of chasing a perfect number, watch for: Thirst: feeling thirsty often = you may need more. Urine colour: pale yellow = usually good; dark yellow = drink more. Symptoms of mild dehydration: dry mouth, headache, dizziness, fatigue.
When you sweat a lot
If you’re working outside, exercising, or riding a motorbike in hot sun: Sip water regularly (every 15–20 minutes) instead of waiting until you’re very thirsty.
For very intense sweating over a long period, an oral rehydration solution or electrolyte drink can help replace salts too (but avoid very sugary drinks).
Don’t overdo it
Avoid forcing large amounts of water in a short period (for example, 1 - 2 litres within an hour). Too much water too quickly can dilute electrolytes and make you feel unwell.
Key idea: In hot weather, drink more than usual, spread it out through the day, watch your urine colour, and increase your intake when you sweat - but don’t chug extreme amounts at once.
Hydration tips while working out
Before your workout
1–2 hours before: drink about 300–500 ml of water. 15–20 minutes before: if you feel thirsty, sip another 100–200 ml. Avoid starting your workout already thirsty or with a very full stomach of water.
During your workout
For light–moderate exercise (under 1 hour): Sip 100–150 ml every 15–20 minutes.
For intense/long workouts (over 1 hour), especially in heat: Keep sipping regularly. Consider a low-sugar electrolyte drink if sweating a lot.👉 Key idea: Sip regularly, don’t wait to be very thirsty.
After your workout
Drink 200–500 ml of water within 30 minutes after finishing.
If your clothes are soaked with sweat, you may need more — sip slowly over the next 1–2 hours. A simple sign: your urine should return to pale yellow later in the day
What to drink
Best choice: Plain water for most workouts.
Electrolyte drinks: Useful if:
You exercise hard for more than 60 minutes.
You sweat heavily.
You’re training in very hot/humid weather.
Avoid very sugary drinks and energy drinks as your main hydration source.
Drinking water when sick (fever, cold)
Why water matters when you’re ill
When your temperature is higher, you sweat more and breathe faster, so your body loses more water
Cold/flu symptoms dehydrate you. Runny nose, rapid breathing, coughing, and especially vomiting or diarrhoea (if present) all draw water out of your body.
Hydration supports your immune system. Water helps your blood carry nutrients and immune cells where they’re needed, and supports your kidneys in removing waste products.
Signs you may not be drinking enough
Dark yellow urine and weeing very little
Very dry mouth and lips
Feeling dizzy, weak, or extremely tired
Headache that feels worse when you stand up
If you notice these signs, try to increase your fluid intake.
What and how to drink
Plain water: main choice, sip often.
Warm liquids: warm water, herbal tea, or clear broth can: Soothe a sore throat, help loosen mucus, make you feel more comfortable
Oral rehydration solution (ORS) or electrolyte drinks: Useful if you have a high temperature, vomiting, or diarrhoea, to replace both water and salts. Avoid very sugary drinks.
👉 Tip: If you don’t feel like drinking much at once, take small sips every few minutes.
When to seek medical help (important)
Drinking water at home helps, but you should see a doctor if:
You can’t keep fluids down (vomit everything)
You feel very dizzy, confused, or have a very fast heartbeat.
You have trouble breathing, chest pain, or a high, lasting fever.
A baby, child, pregnant woman, or elderly person shows signs of dehydration.
Hydration doesn’t cure the illness, but it helps your body recover better.
Hydration tips for office workers, students, and drivers
Office workers
Keep a water bottle on your desk where you can see it.
Drink a small glass (150–250 ml) every 1–2 hours.
Use “triggers”: drink when you check emails, finish a task, or return from a break.
Limit coffee and sugary drinks; let plain water be your main choice.
Students
Start the day with a small glass of water before leaving for school.
Carry a reusable bottle and sip during breaks between classes.
When studying for long periods, drink a few sips every 30–45 minutes.
Don’t rely on energy drinks or very sweet tea as your main fluids.
Drivers
Drink water before heading out on long drives to ensure you\'re properly hydrated.
Always carry a water bottle while driving, especially on long trips
On long drives, stop every 2 hours to stretch and hydrate. This can help prevent fatigue and improve concentration on the road.
Choose water for longer-lasting hydration instead of sugary drinks and caffeine.
Even on short trips, sip water to maintain energy and alertness, especially if you\'re driving for an extended period.
Seasonal & Situational Content
SmartDrink AI
Health Benefits
Healthier Skin
Seasonal
Explore
Only paying attention to how much water you drink, not how your urine looks
Practical Tips
